about.intro: I am an AI Automation Specialist from Hyderabad, India, passionate about building intelligent systems that transform how businesses operate. I specialize in designing and deploying AI chatbots, voice bots, and automation workflows that streamline processes and boost productivity.
about.description: With hands-on experience creating 60+ AI chatbots and 5+ voice bots, I help companies automate customer support, HR operations, and internal workflows. I also manage CRM automations using GoHighLevel, ensuring seamless client interactions. Beyond automation, I create tech content on YouTube and Instagram to help others learn and grow in the AI space.

projects.title: Virtual Medical Assistant System
projects.impact:
Handled 50+ calls/day per staff with consistent routing and automation reliability
Enabled full control over call workflows and CRM-driven automation
Reduced call infrastructure cost by ~30% through system consolidation
projects.problem:
Healthcare operations required full control over call handling, routing logic, and CRM-driven automation workflows, which was not achievable with external call platforms like JustCall.

The primary need was to:
- Enable configurable call routing, masking, and forwarding
- Integrate deeply with CRM workflows and trigger-based automations
- Support scalable multi-client operations with isolated environments
- Allow rapid modification of workflows based on operational needs

The organization was already operating extensively within GoHighLevel across multiple client sub-accounts, making it a natural choice to consolidate call operations within the same ecosystem.

This allowed cost optimization through tool consolidation while enabling deeper control over workflows, routing logic, and CRM-driven automation.
projects.hypothesis: A centralized voice automation system built on a configurable CRM platform can provide full control over call workflows, enable event-driven automation, and support scalable multi-client operations while improving operational efficiency.
projects.architecture:
Inbound Call (GHL Number)
→ Telephony Layer (GHL / Twilio)
→ Voice Processing (Retell AI - STT + Conversational Handling)
→ Decision Engine (Workflow Triggers + Conditional Logic)
→ Execution Layer (Routing / Forwarding / Masking / Recording)
→ CRM Layer (Contact + Call Data Capture)
→ Automation Layer (Event-Driven Actions & Notifications)
→ Fail-Safe Layer (Voicemail, Missed Calls, Escalation)
projects.implementation_summary:
Designed and deployed a workflow-driven voice automation system using GoHighLevel as the central orchestration layer, integrating telephony, CRM, and automation workflows into a unified system.

Structured the system for multi-client scalability using sub-account isolation, enabling independent configurations while maintaining a reusable architecture pattern.

Focused on reliability through workflow-based routing logic, fail-safe handling, and systematic logging.
ai_safety: Implemented controlled conversational flows instead of fully open-ended LLM responses
Designed deterministic decision paths for critical healthcare interactions
Added fallback handling for low-confidence or failed AI responses
Logged failed and edge-case conversations for review and system improvement
Structured interactions to minimize unpredictable outputs in sensitive workflows
outcome: ~30% reduction in call infrastructure cost through system consolidation
Handled 50+ calls/day per staff with consistent routing and automation reliability
Enabled full control over call workflows and CRM-driven automation
projects.system_design:
System Design Decisions

Workflow-Oriented Architecture:
System designed around event-driven workflows instead of static call flows, enabling dynamic routing and automation.

Platform Selection (GoHighLevel):
Chosen for its ability to unify telephony, CRM, and automation workflows in a single controllable layer, reducing fragmentation across tools.

Multi-Client Isolation Strategy:
Used GHL sub-accounts to implement logical isolation between clients:
- Independent CRM data
- Separate workflows and triggers
- Dedicated phone configurations
This ensured scalability without cross-client data leakage.

Call Routing Strategy:
Implemented conditional routing based on:
- Staff availability
- Time windows
- Call type / patient context
Ensured efficient distribution without overloading individual staff.

Parallel Handling Design:
Enabled multiple staff members (5+) to receive and handle calls simultaneously without collision using workflow-based routing logic.

Fail-Safe Mechanisms:
Designed fallback systems to handle failure scenarios:
- Voicemail when no staff available
- Missed call recovery workflows
- Escalation paths for unresolved cases

AI Integration Strategy:
Used controlled conversational flows instead of fully autonomous AI responses to maintain predictability and safety in healthcare interactions.

Extensibility Planning:
System structured to allow future integration of AI voice agents for 24/7 automated call handling.

projects.title: AI Healthcare Data Extraction Pipeline
projects.impact:
60% reduction in manual data entry effort
70% improvement in operational productivity
~95% extraction accuracy across real-world pharmacy datasets
projects.problem:
Healthcare staff received patient documents from clients through EHR/EMR portals as scanned files — fax scans and similar formats. Each page contained one patient record with required fields including provider name, chart ID, member ID, insurance name, plan code, date of service, and additional fields specific to the workflow.

Because the documents were scanned, staff could not copy-paste data into their EMR/EHR systems. Every field had to be manually typed, one patient at a time. At scale — 100 to 1,000+ pages per document batch — this was time-intensive, error-prone, and created a significant operational bottleneck with no path to scaling without increasing headcount.
projects.hypothesis:
If an AI system could read each scanned page individually and extract the required fields — at minimum 5 of 7 target fields per patient record — then the staff could copy-paste structured data directly into EMR/EHR portals instead of manually typing every field. The key technical constraint was handling large document volumes (100–1,000+ pages) without context loss.

Solution approach: process documents as independent single-page units, extract structured fields using a vision model, and aggregate results into a CSV with success and failure tagging per page.
projects.architecture:
Document Upload (Multi-page PDF)
→ Page-Level Chunking (1 page = 1 processing unit)
→ Gemini Vision API (OCR + Field Identification per page)
→ Structured Prompt Processing (Field extraction schema enforcement)
→ Output Aggregation (Success rows + Failed rows with page reference)
→ CSV / Excel Export (Copy-paste ready for EMR/EHR workflows)
→ UI Layer (Form-style display matching portal field layout)
projects.system_design:
Page-Level Chunking Strategy:
The core architectural decision. Instead of sending entire documents to the model — which would exceed context limits and lose accuracy across large batches — each page was isolated and processed independently. This enabled reliable extraction at 1,000+ page scale without context degradation.

Model Selection (Gemini Vision API):
Selected for its vision capability to handle scanned document formats that standard text-based LLMs cannot process. Used a Tier 1 API key for production-grade rate limits and HIPAA-aware data handling — the free tier was insufficient for volume and compliance requirements.

Failure Handling Design:
Rather than failing silently, the system flagged failed extractions with the page number and patient identifier in the output CSV. Staff could immediately identify which records needed manual review without re-processing the entire batch.

Output Schema Design:
Structured prompts enforced a consistent field extraction schema across variable document formats. Output was formatted as copy-paste-ready rows matching the field layout of the EMR/EHR portals staff were working in — minimizing the effort between extraction and data entry.

UI Design Decision:
Built a form-style UI that mirrored the layout of the client portals. This reduced cognitive load for staff — instead of reading a raw CSV, they saw extracted data presented in the same format as the system they were pasting into.

AI-Assisted Development Approach:
Used AI-assisted development tools to accelerate implementation of the document processing pipeline, prompt design, and UI components, while retaining full control over system logic, data flow, and architectural decisions.

Tradeoff Consideration:
Chose independent page-level processing over full-document context to ensure scalability and reliability, accepting potential loss of cross-page contextual understanding in favor of consistent extraction accuracy at high volume.
failure_handling: Designed page-level isolation to prevent full pipeline failure when individual pages fail
Implemented success/failure tagging per page to allow partial completion instead of full batch rejection
Considered API rate limits and structured processing in batches to avoid throttling
Handled inconsistent document formats using strict field extraction schemas
Planned retry strategy for failed pages instead of reprocessing entire documents
Ensured system usability even with partial extraction success through human-in-the-loop fallback
ai_safety: Structured prompts with explicit field schemas to reduce hallucination and enforce consistent output format
Failure rows explicitly flagged rather than silently skipped — every failed extraction traceable by page number
Manual validation checkpoints retained for extracted fields before EMR/EHR entry
HIPAA-aware API usage throughout — Tier 1 Gemini API key selected specifically for compliance requirements
No automated write-back to client portals — human review maintained as the final step before data entry
System designed to assist staff, not replace human judgment in patient data handling
outcome: ~90% field coverage across variable document formats
Successfully processed 1,000+ page document batches in production
Deployed to company production environment with active team usage
projects.implementation_summary:
Built and deployed an end-to-end AI document extraction application that processed 1,000+ page pharmacy document batches by chunking each page individually and sending it through the Gemini Vision API for field extraction. Output was aggregated into structured CSV files with success and failure rows, formatted for direct use in EMR/EHR workflows.

Designed a form-style UI matching client portal layouts to minimize staff effort between extraction and data entry. Deployed to company production environment with active team usage achieving 60% reduction in manual data entry effort and 70% productivity improvement.

Further automation — direct portal write-back — was scoped but deferred due to client HIPAA policy constraints and client readiness, documented as a future extension of the system.

System designed to support incremental improvements without reprocessing entire datasets, enabling efficient iteration and operational stability.

projects.title: AI Chatbots for Operations
projects.impact:
~90% response accuracy achieved across production chatbot systems
60+ chatbots deployed across healthcare, HR, billing, IT, immigration, and internal operations
~30% cost reduction through platform migration from FastBots to Chatbase
projects.context: This project involved operating, optimizing, and scaling a multi-domain chatbot infrastructure built on Chatbase and FastBots (white-labeled platforms). The role here was not platform development — it was AI systems ownership: knowledge base architecture, prompt engineering, evaluation loop design, client-specific customization, and continuous performance improvement across 60+ production bots.
projects.problem:
Healthcare and enterprise clients required scalable, always-available query resolution across multiple departments and use cases — staff training support, client onboarding, website lead generation, and internal operations. Dependency on human trainers and support staff was creating bottlenecks, inconsistent information delivery, and scaling constraints as client count grew.

Each client had unique domain knowledge, compliance requirements, and operational contexts that required individually structured and maintained chatbot systems — not a one-size-fits-all deployment.
projects.hypothesis: Knowledge-base-driven chatbot systems with domain-specific structured datasets, optimized prompts, and continuous evaluation loops can deliver accurate, scalable, and reliable responses across production environments — replacing or reducing dependency on human trainers and support staff.
projects.architecture:
User Query
→ Chatbase / FastBots Interface (White-labeled, client-branded)
→ Structured Knowledge Base (Domain-specific PDFs, website links, training materials, meeting data)
→ LLM Response Generation
→ Evaluation Layer (Response review, failure logging, accuracy tracking)
→ Optimization Loop (Prompt updates, KB additions, retraining cycles)
projects.system_design:
Knowledge Base Architecture per Client:
Each chatbot received a domain-specific knowledge base built from raw unstructured client data — PDFs, official website content, training materials, meeting recordings, and operational forms. Raw data was organized and structured before upload to maximize retrieval accuracy. Generic or mixed datasets were explicitly avoided to prevent cross-domain contamination.

Prompt Engineering Strategy:
Designed structured, HIPAA-aware prompts specific to each client and their departments. Prompts were not generic — each was tuned to the domain, use case, and compliance requirements of the specific client. Continuous iteration based on failure patterns and low-confidence responses.

Evaluation Loop Design:
Built ongoing evaluation cycles — not one-time deployments. Each bot was monitored for failed responses, low-confidence answers, and out-of-scope queries. Findings fed directly back into prompt updates and KB expansions. This is what drove accuracy from initial deployment levels to ~90%.

Platform Migration Decision (FastBots → Chatbase):
Identified that Chatbase offered better management, scalability, and cost efficiency for the infrastructure scale being operated. Led migration across the bot portfolio, achieving ~30% cost reduction while improving operational control.

Multi-Use-Case Deployment:
Same infrastructure adapted for different client needs — staff training, website lead generation, onboarding support, and general query resolution — by adjusting knowledge base scope and prompt behavior per deployment context.
failure_handling: Monitored and logged failed and low-confidence responses across all production bots
Identified data gaps from failed queries and routed back to client leads or management for knowledge base updates
Prompt tuning specifically targeted out-of-scope responses and hallucination patterns
Responses restricted to dataset context only — bots designed to acknowledge knowledge limits rather than fabricate answers
Continuous KB updates maintained accuracy as client operations and information evolved
ai_safety: Responses restricted to structured dataset context — no open-ended generation beyond knowledge base scope
HIPAA-aware prompt design for all healthcare client deployments
Explicit prompt instructions to handle unknown queries with acknowledgment rather than hallucinated responses
Monitoring and logging of failed and low-confidence responses across production systems
Client-specific isolation — each bot operated on its own dataset with no cross-client data access
outcome: ~90% response accuracy across production bots through iterative prompt and KB optimization
Eliminated trainer dependency for multiple clients — chatbots handling staff queries, onboarding, and training support independently
~30% cost reduction through strategic platform migration from FastBots to Chatbase
Chatbots deployed across healthcare, HR, billing, immigration, and internal operations
Company website chatbot functioning as lead generator, general query resolver, and pharmacy field guidance tool
Clients used bots for staff training, website deployment, and lead generation — multiple use cases from single infrastructure
projects.implementation_summary:
Operated and optimized a portfolio of 60+ production chatbot systems across healthcare, HR, billing, immigration, and internal operations. Core work involved knowledge base architecture, domain-specific prompt engineering, evaluation loop management, and continuous performance improvement cycles.

Managed client-specific deployments from data collection through production — sourcing content from official websites, training materials, meeting recordings, and operational documents, then structuring and uploading per bot. Identified and executed platform migration from FastBots to Chatbase, reducing operational costs by ~30% while improving management and scalability.

Bots served multiple use cases across the client base: staff training support, onboarding, website lead generation, and general query resolution — demonstrating that a well-architected knowledge base and prompt system can serve diverse operational needs from a single infrastructure model.

projects.title: Enterprise LMS and AI Support System for Workforce Training
projects.impact:
100% completion rate across all mandatory compliance courses
450+ employees trained across the full organization
Full elimination of live trainer dependency
projects.problem:
Training delivery across the organization and for client staff was manual, inconsistent, and entirely dependent on live trainer availability. Completion tracking was unreliable, content quality varied by session, and the system could not scale as employee and client count grew.

Existing courses were hosted on Teachable — a paid per-course platform that created recurring cost constraints and limited branding control. Content was built from scratch by aggregating data across multiple sources: client documentation, management inputs, team lead knowledge, official regulatory sources, internet research, and even operational WhatsApp communications — reflecting how fragmented and undocumented the organizational knowledge base actually was.

The core problem: no centralized, scalable, self-serve training infrastructure existed. Every training event required human coordination, and there was no reliable way to enforce or verify completion at scale.
projects.hypothesis: A centralized LMS with automated delivery, completion tracking, certification, and an AI support layer can standardize training across the organization, improve completion rates, eliminate live trainer dependency, and reduce training costs — while remaining scalable as headcount and client count grows.
projects.architecture:
Employee Login
→ GoHighLevel Membership Portal (Custom domain, company-branded)
→ Course Content Delivery (Structured modules, assignments, assessments)
→ Automated Completion Tracking + Certification (CEO and VP signed)
→ AI Chatbot Support Layer (Post-training query resolution)
→ Escalation Path (Unresolved queries routed to relevant leads)
projects.system_design:
Platform Migration Decision (Teachable → GoHighLevel):
Teachable was functional but created recurring per-course costs and limited branding flexibility. GoHighLevel offered course hosting effectively at no additional cost within the existing subscription, while enabling full branding control, custom domain setup, and community integration. Migration was not straightforward — required complete course reconstruction, UI redesign using GHL's drag-and-drop builder, custom domain configuration, and community setup. No coding involved but significant architectural planning was required to ensure zero content loss across 8-10 courses.

Knowledge Base Construction Strategy:
Course content was built from a wide variety of sources — client documentation, management and team lead inputs, regulatory sources, internet research, and operational communications. This multi-source aggregation was necessary because no single source contained complete, structured training content. Raw data was organized, structured, and converted into course format before upload.

Certification System Design:
Built a professional certification system with automated issuance upon course completion, signed by the CEO and VP. This added organizational credibility to completions and created a trackable record of trained staff — something the previous manual system could not provide.

Compliance-First Course Design:
Mandatory courses (HIPAA, HR Fundamentals, POSH, Legal) were designed with completion enforcement as the primary constraint. 100% completion was not accidental — it was the result of structured delivery, mandatory assessment gates, and automated tracking that made non-completion visible.

AI Support Layer Integration:
Integrated domain-specific AI chatbots as an always-available post-course support layer. Staff with queries after completing courses could get instant answers without depending on trainers or managers. This reduced the post-training support burden significantly and kept the system fully self-serve.

Recurring Infrastructure Design:
System built as a one-time investment with recurring operational value. Once courses were built and deployed, delivery, tracking, and certification ran automatically. New courses could be added within the same infrastructure without rebuilding the system.
ai_safety: Chatbot responses restricted to course-related and domain-specific content — no open-ended responses beyond training scope
HIPAA and compliance course chatbots explicitly scoped to avoid providing legal or medical advice beyond training material
Monitoring of unresolved and escalated queries to identify knowledge gaps for future course updates
Controlled knowledge scope per course domain to prevent cross-domain confusion in staff responses
outcome: 100% completion rate across all mandatory compliance courses (HIPAA, HR Fundamentals, POSH, Legal)
~85%+ completion rate across service onboarding programs
450+ employees trained across the full organization
Full elimination of live trainer dependency for both compliance and onboarding tracks
15+ courses and assignments built covering healthcare, immigration, IT, HR, and legal domains
Successful migration of 8-10 courses from Teachable to GoHighLevel with zero content loss
Significant cost savings — POSH, HR, and Legal courses replaced paid Udemy/Coursera subscriptions entirely
Recurring training infrastructure built once, operational as long as GHL subscription is maintained
projects.implementation_summary:
Built and migrated a full organizational LMS from Teachable to GoHighLevel, covering 15+ courses and assignments across healthcare, immigration, IT, HR, and legal domains. Handled end-to-end migration including content reconstruction, custom domain setup, branding, community design, assessment configuration, and certification system build — all using GHL's no-code drag-and-drop environment.

Deployed mandatory compliance courses achieving 100% completion across 450+ employees, and service onboarding courses achieving 85%+ completion — eliminating live trainer dependency entirely for both tracks. POSH, HR, and Legal courses replaced paid external subscriptions, delivering direct cost savings to the organization.

Integrated AI chatbots as a post-training support layer, ensuring staff had always-available query resolution without human intervention. System operates as recurring infrastructure — built once, continuously delivering training value as long as the platform subscription is maintained.
